title: EBM/GA2M + Benchmark Evaluation Protocol Survey
summary: Comprehensive survey covering InterpretML EBM/GA2M API (constructor parameters, methods, fitted attributes, FAST interaction detection), Grinsztajn 2022 tabular benchmark protocol (45 datasets across 4 OpenML suites, random search tuning, dataset selection criteria), interpretability metrics for tree-based and additive models (split arity, path length, total splits, number of terms), and Friedman/Nemenyi statistical testing methodology with autorank and scikit-posthocs implementations. Synthesized into a complete evaluation protocol for comparing 6 methods across 5-8 classification-numerical benchmark datasets.

step1_interpretml_ebm_api.title: InterpretML EBM API Reference

feature_types.description: auto, quantile, rounded_quantile, uniform, continuous, nominal, or custom bins

max_bins.description: Max number of bins per feature for main effects stage

max_interaction_bins.description: Max number of bins per feature for interaction terms

interactions.description: Interaction count specification. '3x' means 3x the number of features. Accepts counts, percentages, or feature index tuples

validation_size.description: Validation set proportion for early stopping

outer_bags.description: Number of bagged models for error bounds

greedy_ratio.description: Proportion of greedy vs cyclic boosting; 0 disables greedy

smoothing_rounds.description: Initial regularization iterations for main effects

max_leaves.description: Maximum tree leaves per boosting step (depth-1 stump)

explanation: explain_local(X, y, name, init_score) - instance-level contribution

fitted_attributes.term_features_: List[tuple] - Feature indices composing each term. Single features = 1-tuples, interactions = 2-tuples
fitted_attributes.bagged_scores_: Multi-dimensional array - Ensemble model outputs from each outer bag
fitted_attributes.bin_weights_: List - Sample mass per term bin
fitted_attributes.link_: str - logit, probit, or other transformation

interpretability_extraction.interaction_terms_count: sum(1 for t in model.term_features_ if len(t) == 2)
interpretability_extraction.interaction_term_names: [model.term_names_[i] for i, t in enumerate(model.term_features_) if len(t) == 2]
interpretability_extraction.importance_note: term_importances computes the average of absolute predicted values per feature across training set

step1_interpretml_ebm_api.minimal_code_example:
from interpret.glassbox import ExplainableBoostingClassifier
from interpret import show

ebm = ExplainableBoostingClassifier()
ebm.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Number of terms
n_terms = len(ebm.term_names_)
print(f'Total terms: {n_terms}')

# Interaction terms
interaction_terms = [ebm.term_names_[i] for i, t in enumerate(ebm.term_features_) if len(t) == 2]
print(f'Interaction terms ({len(interaction_terms)}): {interaction_terms}')

# Feature importances
importances = ebm.term_importances('avg_weight')

# Global explanation
ebm_global = ebm.explain_global(name='EBM')
show(ebm_global)

# Local explanation
ebm_local = ebm.explain_local(X_test, y_test, name='EBM')
show(ebm_local)

step2_fast_algorithm.title: FAST Algorithm and GA2M Interaction Detection
step2_fast_algorithm.algorithm_overview: FAST (Feature Appropriate Scoring for Tabular data) is a computationally efficient method for ranking all O(d^2) pairwise feature interactions as candidates for inclusion in a GA2M model. Introduced by Lou, Caruana, Gehrke, and Hooker at KDD 2013.
algorithm_steps: Step 1 - Train Main Effects (GAM): Fit all univariate terms f_i(x_i) via cyclic gradient boosting with round-robin feature training. Each boosting step trains a depth-1 tree (stump) on a single feature using very low learning rate (0.015) so feature order is irrelevant.
Step 2 - Compute Residuals: After main effects converge, compute residuals r = y - (intercept + sum of f_i(x_i)). These residuals capture variance unexplained by additive main effects.
Step 3 - Discretize Features: Discretize each feature into b equi-frequency bins (typically b <= 256). This reduces the scoring cost per pair from O(n*log(n)) to O(b^2 + n).

Step 4 - Score All Pairs: For all O(d^2) feature pairs (x_i, x_j), fit a shallow tree (depth-2) to predict residuals r using only features (x_i, x_j). Score each pair by residual sum of squares (RSS) reduction. Lower RSS = stronger interaction signal.

Step 5 - Rank and Select Pairs: Rank all pairs by their RSS score. The FAST algorithm is run on each of the outer_bags (default 14), giving k rankings per pair, which are aggregated. Select top-k pairs where k is the 'interactions' parameter (default '3x' = 3 * n_features for classifier, '5x' for regressor).

Step 6 - Refit Full Model: Include selected interaction terms f_{i,j}(x_i, x_j) in the additive model. Refit the complete model including both main effects and interactions via cyclic gradient boosting. Spurious interactions (from correlated features) diminish automatically as boosting proceeds.

computational_complexity.per_pair_scoring: O(b^2 + n) per pair with b bins (typically b <= 256)
computational_complexity.total_ranking: O(d^2 * (b^2 + n)) for ranking all pairs
computational_complexity.comparison: FAST is 3-4 orders of magnitude faster than ANOVA and Grove methods for interaction detection
computational_complexity.practical_note: FAST is not sensitive to bin size within a wide range

key_properties.detection_method: Residual-based statistical ranking using shallow trees on discretized features
key_properties.selection_strategy: Greedy forward selection of top-k pairs based on aggregated RSS across outer bags
key_properties.spurious_handling: Spurious interactions from correlated features are down-weighted during refitting via gradient boosting

FAST_residual_based.detection_method: Residual-based RSS scoring of shallow trees on feature pairs
FAST_residual_based.structural_analysis: None - ranks pairs independently with no graph structure
FAST_residual_based.group_detection: No - only detects pairwise interactions, no higher-order groups
FAST_residual_based.signed_information: No - uses unsigned residual reduction only
FAST_residual_based.output: Ranked list of top-k feature pairs

co_information_spectral.structural_analysis: Signed graph + spectral clustering (SPONGE) reveals interaction structure
co_information_spectral.computational_cost: O(d^2 * n) for pairwise CI computation + O(d^3) for spectral decomposition
co_information_spectral.group_detection: Yes - spectral clustering identifies synergistic groups of features
co_information_spectral.signed_information: Yes - distinguishes synergistic (positive CI) from redundant (negative CI) interactions
co_information_spectral.output: Feature clusters with signed interaction structure, guiding oblique split construction

step3_grinsztajn_benchmark.title: Grinsztajn et al. 2022 Benchmark Protocol
step3_grinsztajn_benchmark.paper: Why do tree-based models still outperform deep learning on typical tabular data?
step3_grinsztajn_benchmark.authors: Leo Grinsztajn, Edouard Oyallon, Gael Varoquaux
dataset_selection_criteria: Heterogeneous columns (not same sensor repeated, excludes images/signals)
Real-world data (not synthetic, but allows simulated data of practical importance like Higgs)
Training set truncated to 10,000 samples for larger datasets
Not too easy (relative difference with default models > 5%)

openml_suites.334: Classification on numerical and categorical features
openml_suites.335: Regression on numerical and categorical features

recommended_classification_numerical_dat.name: clf_num_california
recommended_classification_numerical_dat.description: California housing adapted for binary classification

recommended_classification_numerical_dat.name: clf_num_Higgs
recommended_classification_numerical_dat.description: Higgs boson signal vs background from Monte Carlo simulations. Training truncated to 10K.

recommended_classification_numerical_dat.name: clf_num_heloc
recommended_classification_numerical_dat.description: Home equity line of credit risk

evaluation_protocol.split_method: Random train/test split with stratification. Training set truncated to 10,000 samples.
evaluation_protocol.hyperparameter_tuning: Random search with ~20,000 compute hours total per learner. Performance evaluated as function of number of random search iterations.
evaluation_protocol.validation: Validation set carved from training data for early stopping and hyperparameter selection
evaluation_protocol.repetitions: 15 shuffles of random search order to estimate variance
evaluation_protocol.default_baselines: Each method's default hyperparameters serve as first random search iteration

step3_grinsztajn_benchmark.dataset_loading_code_openml:
import openml
SUITE_ID = 337  # Classification, numerical features
benchmark_suite = openml.study.get_suite(SUITE_ID)
for task_id in benchmark_suite.tasks:
    task = openml.tasks.get_task(task_id)
    dataset = task.get_dataset()
    X, y, categorical_indicator, attribute_names = dataset.get_data(
        dataset_format='dataframe',
        target=dataset.default_target_attribute
    )
step3_grinsztajn_benchmark.dataset_loading_code_huggingface:
from datasets import load_dataset
dataset = load_dataset('inria-soda/tabular-benchmark', 'clf_num_electricity')
X = dataset['train'].to_pandas()

step4_interpretability_metrics.title: Interpretability and Accuracy Metrics

total_splits.description: Total number of splits summed across all trees in the ensemble. Lower = simpler.
total_splits.for_FIGS: model.complexity_ gives the total number of rules/splits
total_splits.for_oblique_trees: Sum of internal nodes across all trees
total_splits.for_EBM: Not directly applicable (uses lookup tables, not tree splits)

average_path_length.description: Mean number of splits from root to leaf, averaged across all trees and samples. Lower = simpler.
average_path_length.computation: Traverse each tree, compute depth of leaf reached for each sample, average
average_path_length.for_FIGS: Traverse model.trees_ to compute average depth

split_arity.description: Average number of features used per split. 1 = axis-aligned, >1 = oblique. Lower = more interpretable.
split_arity.for_oblique: Count features with non-zero coefficients per split node, average across all splits

max_split_arity.description: Maximum number of features in any single split. Worst-case interpretability measure.

number_of_terms_ebm.description: Total univariate + interaction terms in EBM. Direct interpretability measure for GAMs.

number_of_interaction_terms_ebm.description: Count of pairwise interaction terms specifically.
number_of_interaction_terms_ebm.computation: sum(1 for t in model.term_features_ if len(t) == 2)

model_size.description: Total number of parameters or lookup table entries.
model_size.for_EBM: Sum of sizes of all term_scores_ arrays
model_size.for_trees: Total number of nodes across all trees

computation_code.ebm_metrics:
# EBM interpretability metrics
from interpret.glassbox import ExplainableBoostingClassifier
ebm = ExplainableBoostingClassifier()
ebm.fit(X_train, y_train)

n_total_terms = len(ebm.term_names_)
n_main_effects = sum(1 for t in ebm.term_features_ if len(t) == 1)
n_interactions = sum(1 for t in ebm.term_features_ if len(t) == 2)
importances = ebm.term_importances('avg_weight')

print(f'Total terms: {n_total_terms}')
print(f'Main effects: {n_main_effects}')
print(f'Interactions: {n_interactions}')

scalability_metrics.wall_clock_time: Total time for full pipeline (info computation + graph analysis + tree fitting)
component_times: Graph construction and spectral clustering time

step4_interpretability_metrics.interpretability_tradeoff_evidence: Recent research (Springer BISE 2024) challenges the strict performance-interpretability tradeoff for tabular data. Advanced interpretable models like EBM, IGANN, and GAMI-Net can achieve high predictive performance while remaining fully interpretable. EBM even surpasses most black-box models on average.

step5_statistical_testing.title: Statistical Testing Protocol for Multi-Dataset Comparison

decision_tree.bayesian_alternative: Bayesian signed-rank test with ROPE (baycomp package)

friedman_nemenyi_protocol: 1. Organize results as matrix: rows = datasets, columns = methods, cells = metric value (e.g. balanced accuracy)
2. Compute ranks per dataset (row): best method gets rank 1, ties get average ranks
3. Compute average ranks across datasets for each method
4. Run Friedman test: scipy.stats.friedmanchisquare(*[col for col in data.T]). If p < 0.05, proceed.
5. Run Nemenyi post-hoc: scikit_posthocs.posthoc_nemenyi_friedman(data). Returns p-value matrix.
6. Compute Critical Difference: CD = q_alpha * sqrt(k*(k+1)/(6*N)) where k = number of methods, N = number of datasets
7. Plot CD diagram: methods on horizontal axis by average rank; crossbars connect methods NOT significantly different

implementation_option_a_autorank.description: Recommended for simplicity. Auto-selects appropriate test based on data characteristics.
implementation_option_a_autorank.code:
from autorank import autorank, plot_stats, create_report, latex_table

# Input: DataFrame with columns = methods, rows = datasets
result = autorank(df, alpha=0.05, verbose=True)
create_report(result)  # text summary
plot_stats(result)     # CD diagram
latex_table(result)    # for paper
auto_selection_logic: Shapiro-Wilk test for normality (with Bonferroni correction)
If all normal + homoscedastic -> repeated measures ANOVA + Tukey HSD
If any non-normal or heteroscedastic -> Friedman + Nemenyi
Two populations: paired t-test (normal) or Wilcoxon signed-rank (non-normal)
implementation_option_a_autorank.input_format: Pandas DataFrame where each column is a method/population, each row is a dataset/observation. All columns must be numeric and equal length.

implementation_option_b_manual.code_friedman:
from scipy.stats import friedmanchisquare
import scikit_posthocs as sp
import numpy as np

# data: numpy array, rows = datasets, columns = methods
stat, p = friedmanchisquare(*[data[:, i] for i in range(data.shape[1])])
print(f'Friedman chi2={stat:.4f}, p={p:.4f}')

if p < 0.05:
    nemenyi = sp.posthoc_nemenyi_friedman(data)
    print(nemenyi)
implementation_option_b_manual.code_cd_diagram:
import scikit_posthocs as sp
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Compute average ranks
avg_rank = data.rank(axis=1).mean(axis=0)

# Run post-hoc test
nemenyi_results = sp.posthoc_nemenyi_friedman(data.values)

# Plot critical difference diagram
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 2), dpi=100)
sp.critical_difference_diagram(avg_rank, nemenyi_results)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('cd_diagram.pdf', bbox_inches='tight')

garcia_herrera_2008_extensions.description: More powerful post-hoc corrections than Nemenyi
garcia_herrera_2008_extensions.holm_procedure: Pairwise Wilcoxon with Holm correction - more power than Nemenyi for control comparisons
garcia_herrera_2008_extensions.recommendation: For up to 9 methods, Bergmann-Hommel has highest power. For more, use Shaffer or Holm.

effect_size_kendall_w.description: Kendall's W (coefficient of concordance) quantifies how consistently methods are ranked across datasets. Report alongside Friedman chi2 and p-value.

bayesian_alternative.description: Computes posterior probabilities: p_left (method A better), p_rope (methods equivalent), p_right (method B better)
bayesian_alternative.rope_definition: Region of Practical Equivalence. Default: (-0.01, 0.01) for accuracy differences. Two classifiers are 'practically equivalent' if difference falls within ROPE.
bayesian_alternative.advantage: Can declare practical equivalence (impossible with NHST). More conservative than frequentist tests.
bayesian_alternative.code:
from baycomp import SignedRankTest
result = SignedRankTest(accuracy_diffs, rope=0.01)
print(f'P(A better): {result.probs()[0]:.3f}')
print(f'P(equivalent): {result.probs()[1]:.3f}')
print(f'P(B better): {result.probs()[2]:.3f}')

cd_diagram_libraries: autorank: plot_stats(result) - simplest, auto-generates from autorank result
hfawaz/cd-diagram: GitHub repo with Wilcoxon-Holm post-hoc CD diagrams

step6_complete_evaluation_protocol.title: Complete Evaluation Protocol Summary

methods_table.tuning_strategy: Grid x Grid: max_rules [4,8,12,16,20] x n_features [2,3,4,5]

evaluation_checklist: 1. Balanced accuracy (5-fold stratified CV, report mean +/- std)
2. AUC-ROC (same CV folds, for binary classification datasets)
3. Total number of splits (for tree-based methods)
4. Average split arity (1 for axis-aligned, >1 for oblique)
7. For EBM: number of terms, number of interaction terms
8. Frustration index (dataset-level, compute once per dataset)
9. Correlation: frustration index vs (oblique accuracy - axis-aligned accuracy)

reporting_requirements.table_1: Accuracy results (mean +/- std) across all datasets and methods
reporting_requirements.table_2: Interpretability metrics across all datasets and methods
reporting_requirements.figure_1: Critical difference diagram for balanced accuracy
reporting_requirements.figure_2: Scatter plot of frustration index vs oblique accuracy gain
reporting_requirements.table_3: Statistical test results (Friedman p-value, Kendall's W, pairwise Nemenyi p-values)

protocol: Run Friedman + Nemenyi separately for each metric
Report Kendall's W effect size alongside p-values
Paired Wilcoxon between signed-spectral (ours) and each baseline for focused comparison
Report both frequentist (Friedman/Nemenyi) and descriptive (mean +/- std across datasets)

max_rules.description: Maximum total number of rules/splits across all trees

min_impurity_decrease.description: Minimum impurity decrease threshold for splits

max_features.description: Number of features to consider for best split

fitted_attributes.trees_: List of root nodes of all trees
fitted_attributes.complexity_: Integer counting total number of rules/splits in the model

answer:
This survey establishes a complete, actionable evaluation protocol for comparing Balance-Guided Oblique Trees against state-of-the-art interpretable models on tabular data, covering five tightly integrated components.

**EBM Baseline Configuration.** The ExplainableBoostingClassifier from InterpretML [1, 3] provides the gold-standard interpretable baseline. Key defaults include interactions='3x' (3 times feature count), max_bins=1024 for mains and 64 for interactions, outer_bags=14 for bagging, learning_rate=0.015, and max_leaves=2 (depth-1 stumps) [1]. After fitting, interpretability is measured via len(model.term_names_) for total terms and counting 2-tuples in model.term_features_ for interaction count [1, 24]. The FAST algorithm underlying EBM's interaction detection [4, 5] works by training main effects first via cyclic gradient boosting, computing residuals, then scoring all O(d^2) feature pairs by fitting shallow trees to residuals on discretized features (O(b^2+n) per pair) [4, 23]. This is fundamentally different from our Co-Information spectral approach: FAST uses greedy residual-based pair ranking with no structural analysis, while our method builds a signed interaction graph enabling spectral clustering of synergistic feature groups [4, 5].

**Benchmark Protocol.** Following Grinsztajn et al. 2022 [7, 8], we select 5-8 classification-numerical datasets from OpenML Suite 337 [8, 9]: electricity (~38K samples, 8 features), california (~21K, 8), Higgs (~98K, 28), jannis (~58K, 54), eye_movements (~7.6K, 23), credit (~17K, 10), MiniBooNE (~73K, 50), and heloc (~10K, 22). Dataset selection criteria require d/n < 1/10, d < 500, no missing data, heterogeneous columns, and real-world origin [7]. Training sets are truncated to 10,000 samples per the benchmark protocol. Evaluation uses 5-fold stratified cross-validation with random search hyperparameter tuning. Metrics include balanced accuracy (primary) and AUC-ROC (secondary for binary) [7].

**Interpretability Metrics.** Beyond accuracy, we measure: total number of splits (FIGS: model.complexity_) [16, 18], average split arity (features per split: 1 for axis-aligned, >1 for oblique) [20, 21], average path length, number of trees (len(model.trees_) for FIGS) [18], and for EBM: number of terms and interaction terms [1]. Recent evidence challenges the strict performance-interpretability tradeoff, showing EBM matches or exceeds black-box models while remaining fully interpretable [19]. The split arity metric is particularly important for oblique trees: bivariate splits (arity=2) retain reasonable interpretability while being competitive with full multivariate splits [21].

**Statistical Testing.** Following Demsar 2006 [10] and Garcia & Herrera 2008 [11], we compare k=6 methods across N=5-8 datasets using the Friedman test followed by Nemenyi post-hoc test. The autorank library [12] automates test selection: Shapiro-Wilk normality check, then Friedman + Nemenyi for non-normal multi-population comparisons. Critical difference diagrams are generated via scikit-posthocs [13, 14] or autorank's plot_stats() [12]. We report Kendall's W effect size (W = chi2/(N*(K-1)), thresholds: 0.1 small, 0.3 moderate, 0.5 large) alongside p-values [25]. For focused pairwise comparisons of our method against each baseline, we use paired Wilcoxon tests with Holm correction [11]. The Bayesian signed-rank test with ROPE provides a complementary analysis capable of declaring practical equivalence [15].

**Complete Protocol.** Six methods are compared: (i) Axis-aligned FIGS (max_rules grid [4,8,12,16,20]), (ii) Random-oblique FIGS, (iii) Hard-threshold SG-FIGS, (iv) Unsigned spectral FIGS, (v) Signed spectral FIGS (ours), (vi) EBM (default settings). The evaluation checklist covers: balanced accuracy, AUC-ROC, total splits, split arity, path length, training time, EBM term counts, frustration index, and frustration-vs-accuracy-gain correlation. Reporting includes accuracy tables (mean +/- std), interpretability metric tables, CD diagrams, frustration index scatter plots, and full statistical test results.
follow_up_questions: What are the exact dataset sizes (n_samples, n_features, n_classes) for each of the 16 classification-numerical datasets in OpenML Suite 337, and which 5-8 best match our 10K-100K sample range hypothesis?
Does FIGS support sample_weight for handling imbalanced datasets, and what is the recommended strategy for class imbalance across the benchmark datasets?

What is the recommended random search budget (number of iterations) for EBM hyperparameter tuning, given that the default configuration already performs well — should we tune EBM or use defaults only?
How should we handle the frustration index computation for datasets with many features (d>20) where the pairwise Co-Information matrix becomes expensive to compute?
